sentences.text: We arrive in Australia on the 3rd of August, 1949.
Yes.
By ship, and the ship's name was
We went to Sydney.
And we were supposed to land in Melbourne, because all our friends were in Melbourne, and we were so happy.
But the ship before us broke down in Fremantle, and our ship was still all right, so we were just taking water, maybe some food to go to Sydney.
And the other ship started.
And I was so heartbroken, because I was so in love with Melbourne.
And there I couldn't-- I couldn't stay there.
And our friends had everything ready for us.
We had to leave and we had to work and everything.
But it wasn't meant to be, so we came to Sydney.
Because the ship-- later on, I could've gone, but who had the finances and all that?
We couldn't just right away-- but the ship didn't-- we were not let off the ship in Melbourne.
And we came to Sydney.
And that was the most roughest piece of journey we had with our-- with the ship, because that-- what is it called-- [INAUDIBLE] you know, around from Melbourne to Sydney.
And we arrived 3rd of August '49 in Sydney.
It was the late afternoon.
We were told later on we will get off the ship and all the children, the sick children, will be taken to hospital.
And Andrew got sick the day before.
All the sick children were registered and he wasn't on the list, so I had to take him with me.
And we were going on a train.
First of all, when I walked down the steps, I kissed the ground, happy to be away so far from Germany.
And then he wasn't well, and I didn't have warm clothes, because we were-- we were told we are going to a tropical-- semi-tropical or tropical country, and all our luggage was not together with us.
But I had a American woolen sleeping bag, and that maybe saved his life, because it was so cold on the train.
August, when we arrived to [INAUDIBLE], it was snow almost to the place where we were going to-- to live.
And the mountain, the Blue Mountain [INAUDIBLE] you know, you could walk out to the mountains.
And he was taken straight away to hospital.
I went to-- to report that he's not well.
And the doctor came.
He looked him over.
And he said, there's no time for ambulance.
He took him with me in the car to the hospital.
And he had measles, pneumonia, and gastroenteritis.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then they didn't find out that he was allergic to penicillin.
After a few injections, he got all rash in his mouth.
He couldn't eat.
He couldn't drink.
They kept him there for 6 weeks.
I stayed there the whole time in--
He was 2 and 1/2.
But my husband-- they were all on contracts, so the government had taken them away for work.
He went to Richmond to work for the Air Force as manual labor.
And I stayed in [INAUDIBLE] 'til Andrew will get better.
And then when he got better, he couldn't even speak.
He couldn't even walk.
But somehow, you know, he recovered pretty well.
And it was-- I went to a camp place, Greta near Maitland.
There were two camps.
One was called the Silver City and one was called the Chocolate City.
I was in the Silver City.
[LAUGHS] There was a film about it all, you know.
And I didn't stay there for very long.
It wasn't too bad.
I even found myself a little bit of a job there too.
I was working while Andrew was in-- in kindergarten.
But it wasn't for long.
I got not so sick, but something was wrong on top of my chest.
They-- they couldn't find out what it was straightaway.
I had five operations.
And when I was in hospital, Andrew had to be in a-- in a-- you know, where they keep children without parents.
What is it called?
The orphanage.
There was nobody to look out.
But he was looked after very well, but it wasn't like being with me.
And later on when I came to Sydney, I was straightaway sent to the North Shore Hospital to see what is wrong, and from there to Crown Street, to the university.
And they found out that because they tried so many times over there in Maitland and Newcastle, they couldn't get to it, what was wrong.
And they found out that I had bone tuberculosis.
And then they put me on streptomycin.

And I was told I have to have those injections as long 'til the would will heal. 3 and 1/2 years I was on injections, because if I interrupt it, the things I had before, it's lost, so I just have to be prepared. 2 years, there was 7 days a week.
And the other 1 and 1/2 year was only 5 days a week.
Sometimes a sister nurse used to come or a doctor or a sister.
I had injections at home.
And when I started work, they came to work and gave me the injections.
I worked in Gladesville.
It was a very beautiful place.
I was making children's clothes.
And that was the only job I could take.
I couldn't go to any other factory.
Something light-- I couldn't work heavy stuff.
But I loved every minute of it.
To make baby's clothes, that was such a joy.
And Andrew was at school, so I was only working school hours.
Took him to school, and going home from school I used to pick him up.
It wasn't easy--
I had one more operation in North Shore Hospital.
And after that, you know, they-- it healed.
Yes, from school and home, so he wouldn't be alone.
But it wasn't easy to work, how shall I say, school hours.
They all then-- they wanted 40 hours a week.
But I managed, you know, to talk them into that.
They allowed me to work less.
[LAUGHS]
Then in 1958, my daughter was born.
We had, then, our own house.
In [INAUDIBLE].
Yes.
And then she was 11 years younger than Andrew.
And I was giving-- from that place, I was giving work home.
She was a very good baby, so I could manage to work almost the same eight hours a day at home.
I had the machines given and all that.
So it was for babies.
It was very light and easy and artistic work, so I really appreciated.
And it helped lot to-- to meet the things when one was building a house and getting the furniture in and all that.
And after a while, everything went all right and then I've heard that I can claim compensation.
Gosh, it took me that long.
And I got from America-- my late cousin told me about Dr.
Hornigbaum in Sydney.
If nobody's going to help you, he will.
So I got in touch with him, and he helped.
He got some-- some lawyers in German.
And he said, you don't have to pay.
If we succeed to win your case, then they'll take 6% of the money I'll get from Germany.
And it worked out that way.
But the way they were trying took me 13 years to get a cent out of them.
But we succeeded.
Dr.
Hornigbaum succeeded.
And then after that, you know, I still were not working there, only a different place.
And 1983-- before that, I was called three times to the Sydney German consulate to give, oh, not statements, but they will ask, do I know that German SS men, and do I not, from from Sobibor?
I knew them by name.
Then one day I was giving evidence, they brought in a very big photo-- photo album, and a lot of Germans and soldiers, SS men.
And I had to find somebody will the name of Gomerski.
He was a drunkard all the time and came with the [INAUDIBLE]-- in where the-- oh, the-- the crematoriums were and the-- and the gas chambers.
And I had to find him, you know, in a photo to see if I recognized him.
I looked over once.
I looked over-- I don't know how many times I looked over.
And I said Gomerski's not in that album.
And later on, they had, you know, for lunch.
We came back.
And the secretary, who was sitting there and typing every word I was saying, she gave me the book.
And she said, look it over quiet now.
They're not here.
You might find him.
Because the photos were numbered, and the names were somewhere else.
Looked and looked and looked, and of a sudden, I found Gomerski.
Even the secretary was clapping.
She was so happy for me that I found him, that I really was in Sobibor, that I didn't make it up.
Later on, it came for Frenzel I had to, and for Bauer, because I did remember some of them.
And after 1983, I didn't want to go to Germany.
They asked me, but I recline.
And then I got letters from Dr.
Schneeweiss, it's my duty.
And-- and I said, I'm not going alone to Germany.
My husband wasn't well already then.
I can't go alone.
They said they will pay for Andrew so Andrew can bring me to Hagen.
That was I had to be there for the 17th of April.
When was Easter time?
April or March?
April.
So I got to to Frankfurt, from Frankfurt to Hagen.
And the next day I went to Hagen court, and I walked into-- it was a big, big foyer in the court.
And there was a man standing a little bit further than that wall or the other wall there.
And I said to Judge Kramer, what is Frenzel doing there-- here?
He looked at me, wanted to introduce me to the translator, and there was-- it was a woman.
And he looked at me.
He said, how do you know that that's Frenzel?
I said, I just recognize him.
He was standing there at the profile, the side, leaning on a cane.
And he had like not a raincoat, but all-weather coat on.
But when I looked at him, the first glance and I recognized him after 40 years.
And he was in civilian clothing, and all I knew him was in military uniform.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they-- well, you know, I don't know how they-- you know, that-- but in court, there was that many questions they asked.
And they had about six prosecutor, that man, and Frenzel had his lawyer.

And that was an awful-- you know, even somebody else said once that if that lawyer would've been dressed in SS uniform, he would not have been better than-- than-- the way the questions and all that he was asking.
And of a sudden, the judge came up.
How did I know that the third camp had 27 people, because I mentioned that my brother was executed there.
And I said, I never said that there were-- I knew how many.
I only knew when they were executed.
How did I know it was-- you know, remember?
I said-- And then I asked him in German, would you forget your mother's birthday?
He said no.
I said, he was executed on my mother's birthday, the 27th of June.
They had it all on paper, everything I said.
And then I said to him, you have it all on paper, on tapes.
And I am getting old, older, old, and you want me to have every detail in my head when you have got it all in black and white?
He apologized.
But before that, there were other incidents in court.
Did I know who was shooting?
Then I came just blankly I said to the translator, I said, do you want him to ask, or shall I ask him?
Would he look back when somebody was shooting at him?
I said, I was running for my life.
And I said that the first-- I forgot to mention it before-- the first step, I was outside that-- the fences, and I said, it's not fresh air, it's free air.
And I couldn't care what will happen anymore.
I got out of it.
And then there was another incident.
Did I see the people, they were-- you know, the naked-- where did I know that the people, the women, were taking in Sobibor to the gas chambers?
I said, there was our toilets, but between the toilets and the barrack, there was a little fence and it was a brush fence.
And when the brushes dried out, we could see through.
And coming back from the toilets, even if we wanted to look or not, but you're always a little bit, you know-- wanted to see things.
And I looked through and I saw the women going there.
Then they had a map of Sobibor on-- you know, like the painters have, like a blackboard, but a big map.
And he gave me something, you know, to go and show him where that brush fence was.
It wasn't-- it was like a gate, very little.
And I looked at that map of Sobibor.
I said, that is not Sobibor.
It doesn't look to me, because I know where the rail was, that was on the left, that was on the right, where the barracks were, where the third camp was, and everything.
But that is not Sobibor.
So he walked down from the big, big thing he was sitting there.
And he said he was so sorry.
Can somebody turn the map around?
[PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] Then they didn't ask many-- many questions later on.
Oh, one more thing.
Why did I, after the war, register myself as-- the lawyer, Frenzel's lawyer-- register myself as Wojciszyn, and not as Feldman?
I said, I didn't have any papers.
I didn't have anything.
Who was going to believe me who I am?
Oh, I said, then maybe I didn't want to be Jewish anymore.
And that point was that he was going to tell that I wasn't Jewish.
I only became Jewish because I wanted compensation, [NON-ENGLISH].
I wanted from the Germans.
And then I told the [INAUDIBLE] and the translator I couldn't speak anymore to them.
I said, could she ask him, would they found a sane man during the war who wasn't Jewish who wanted to be Jewish?
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] What happened to Frenzel?
After-- I don't know exactly now.
I'll tell you the truth, I don't know if I care anymore, because everybody was called back to give me-- he was declining.
He wasn't well.
He was nearly 80 years old and all that.
Oh, I was-- wasn't there alone, because somebody came to-- two Dutch people, who knew they were very interested in Sobibor.
And one, Jules Schelvis, he was a few hours in Sobibor, his wife, his whole family, was a Dutchman.
He's a Dutchman.
He-- they all perished in Sobibor.
And he was so interested.
And then there was another friend, Dunya Braur.
She is a writer and she wanted to write so very badly a book about Sobibor.
She even could speak Russian.
She even went to Russia to interview Pechersky.
She's got it all on type.
And Jules wrote a book about Sobibor, not only one, a few more in all.
That is Jules Schelvis.
S-C-H-E-L-V-Y-S, I think.
I think it's like that.
Jules, 'yools,' Jules.
Oh, yes.
He came to Hagen.
And he came with flowers.
And he thought, you know, he knew there is somebody from Australia, and he's got a sister in Melbourne.
He was so anxious.
Every person who came to Hagen as a witness for Sobibor, he came over from Holland to be with them.
And he was a good-- and Dunya, too-- they were both marvelous, marvelous people.
They helped me a little bit to get over every day.
And then they had a Easter break.
And I got away from Frankfurt to Holland, to Amsterdam.
And Jules picked me up.
He lives in Tricht.
And I spent Pesach, half the Pesach.
The last days of Pesach I spent with them.
And then I went back to Germany.
And when that was over, they paid for everything.
They paid the fares when I was flying Lufthansa business class.
They arranged everything, you know.
Later, yes.
Yes.
When I was in Holland a second time, they made a film about the escape.
And they wanted me to be there for the-- the premiere.
And later when I-- that time was the strike, the airplane strikes was-- when was it? '85?
Yes.
And then I couldn't go back, because five weeks I had to-- instead of two weeks, I stayed there for five.
And Jules said, how about we go to Poland?
And he wanted to go to see places and definite to Sobibor.
But I didn't go home then.
Oh, yes.
Yes.
I went to Sobibor.
I found the same-- not as things what I found 18 months ago for the 50th year anniversary of the escape.
But there still was one tower still standing, and the heap of ashes and whatever it is [INAUDIBLE].
There's like a big bed in front of it.
There were flowers, always were planted flowers-- I don't know who was doing that-- and a very big memorial.
And we brought over with us candles and flowers and all that we put there.
But Jules had a video recorder, so he had it all-- all on the video.
I've got it too on the video.
He made me a copy of it.
But 18 months ago, I went with my family for the 50th anniversary of the escape.
And that was organized.
The commemoration was beautifully, beautifully done.
First the entrance with the flags there, Israeli flags and the Polish flags.
And the Polish military had it all arranged with a band, and they had from America a hazzan and a rabbi.
And there was other American.
The one people who-- they are on the international committee who preserves war places, where they wouldn't be demolished and all that.
And it was a lot-- a lot of other-- from other countries people.
And wreath laying was almost all from Warsaw, the ambassadors, not the ambassadors themselves, but they were sending there with wreaths were laid there.
And then there was the gun salute.
And the orchestra was playing all the time, the drums, whenever somebody was going there with the flowers to lay on the graves, because there isn't-- there aren't any graves.
But where the camp was, the Germans demolished everything, and they planted trees.
And it's a forest now.
There's not much clearing.
But the rail is still there and the station is still there.
But from the mines station, there was a little bit on the side where the rails used to-- where the transports used to come to Sobibor, not from the mines-- not on the mines station.
Oh, yes.
Yes, yes.
Yes.
I had Esther Raab was there.
She was one who was in.
And there was Samuel Lehrer.
They are from the United States.
And then there was the one who organized it.
And he has written more than one book.
He was such a young boy that-- when he got there.
And he is Thomas Blatt.
He was there.
And a gentleman--
Yes.
Yes.
No.
No.
No.
He--
No.
No, no.
That was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Leon Feldhendler.
It wasn't Thomas Blatt.
Thomas Blatt was just, you know, the-- an inmate just like we all were.
And then but he helped a lot with the organization, so from Warsaw, we got a special bus there and all that.
It was really organized.
And then the mayor of Chelm was giving a dinner, but I didn't go.
I had something else in Lublin to attend.
And it was really, you know-- Then when I went home, from Lublin to-- to Chelm--
I went to Siedliszcze.
The house is not there anymore, because the-- the town took that place over.
And they had tools there from-- I don't know how to say it, like, material for buildings and tools from the town-- [INAUDIBLE] or something like that.
I don't know exactly how to say that in English because--
Council store place, something like that.
And--
Even it wasn't the same there anymore.
They had a road in the middle.
And the road was somewhere else.
There was only a little bit of a fence in the front.
And I--
Oh, yes, the neighbor was still there.
Even man came over and he said that he remembered my father.
He was such a good man.
He helped him a lot.
And I managed to met-- meet somebody from school, a school friend.
Yes, and she, you know-- we used from-- not from-- from first class we attended school together.
And she lived in Warsaw, but her father's property-- they had a very big mill still there, and she wanted to do it up a bit.
And it was such by chance I was asking for her, you know, somebody knows from the Zhukowskis, are they still there?
And there was a man, she said, yes, Regina Zhukowski came over from Warsaw only yesterday.
And I went there, and we didn't recognize each other.
But we knew who we were.
Yes.
And then I went to the school.
I had photographs made from the school where I used to attend.
The school is still standing, but the town's not the same anymore.
I couldn't recognize the streets.
It was almost unbelievable, what-- during the 40 years Communism or more, what can happen to places.
Even Lublin and Chelm, oh gosh, it wasn't the same.
It wasn't looked after.
The materials were not there.
And even if it was there, it was stolen.
There were big buildings, like-- like a hotel was a four-story-- a hotel was built in Chelm.
And after five years, they didn't allow anybody there.
It was crumbling.
So Communist way.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] when-- what was I?
I was going to-- no, I don't remember now.
Maybe if you ask me another question, it would come to me.
I wanted to tell-- say something from-- still from the camp, but maybe it wasn't important.
Maybe something to do with what reminded you when you were at the ceremony?
No.
No.
No.
The ceremony was-- you know, I've got a video of it in Polish.
And Thomas Blatt sent that video.
It's translated in-- in English.
He survived.
Oh, yes, he survived.
But he passed away maybe four or five years ago.
He wasn't very well and he was an elder gentlemen.
But when I was in Holland, his wife came and his daughter and son-in-law.
I met them.
And they knew about me, because Dunya probably told them.
I met them in Holland.
Dunya Braur.
Dunya, it's D-- maybe Dunya will be [INAUDIBLE]-- yes, I can write them down for you.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Oh gosh.
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] D-U-N-Y-A, that's Dunya, Braur, B-R-A-U-R, Braur.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I still am in contact with them.
I still get all the books that they write, and even they are in Dutch, because her mother was in Mauthausen or in a camp politically where their father was shot, but her mother survived.
She was only eight months old.
And then her mother is an artist, painter.
And everything that happened, you know, she's still alive.
She just turned 80.
And when I was in Holland, I met her.
And it was really, you know, rewarding to be in Holland, you know.
And I've been back to Holland now.
When we went to Sobibor, I made a point to stay at least a week in Holland.
Oh, Jules, yes, and Dunya and Andrew.
Andrew has met Jules before when I was in Germany.
And he met Dunya, too.
But my other kids, daughter-in-law and grandson, they didn't, you know, And my daughter didn't know him, and Peter, so--
Yes.
Yes.
Yes.
Oh, Marie.
[LAUGHS] My daughter's name is Marie and she still keeps her maiden name, Zielinski.
Yes.
Yes.

I didn't know then, but when the museum was starting-- they were building the museum, and when I saw it when it was finished, and I got there for the opening, and then I knew the purpose of my survival.
Yes.
Which is the museum.
To tell the memorial and to tell-- I know it's hundred of us, but every one of us has got different and we've got the same, you know, just to survive, because it's that animal instinct to survive.
And when one is young, it's different.
I don't know if I would have survived now or when my mother-- she was only 42, but still.

You know, when one is young-- I never felt the words-- when my brother said, let's say goodbye, because we won't see, 'til I had my own children-- what it means to be a mother and taking the children to their death.
It wasn't easy.
It's still not easy, but we just have to carry on.
Yes.
Thank you.
Thank you very much for having me.
That is the diamond I took out from my great-grandmother's earring, which survived in my lining of my coat.
After the war I discovered it.
No.
I didn't.
Put it in with other-- my jewelry.
But we had to give it all up when we were taken to Sobibor.
And that somehow got out.
I don't know, it was ripped up, got out, and it got hooked on there.
I didn't know about it.
Yes.
And when the jeweler looked at the diamond, he said, that's at least 150 years old.
